text:
As I noted at the time, Putin's decision to invade Ukraine triggered a somewhat familiar sense of solidarity in the Western world, one reminiscent of the collective outrage following the first Gulf war. However, there was never any real expectation that other regions would naturally fall into line and rally in lockstep with Washington and other major Western capitals over the broader Ukrainian and related conflicts. As I discussed last month, in excess of 40 UN member countries scattered from sub-Saharan Africa to the Indian and Pacific Oceans to the Middle East and to Latin America generally declined to vote in favor of any General Assembly or Security Council resolutions criticizing Russian actions in Ukraine, with about half of those either abstaining from a key vote on the council, or voting against motions expressing what one might euphemistically call diplomatic discontent with the way in which Putin has chosen to address issues affecting the region. And last month also saw the number of non-Western nations that voted against removing Russia from the UN Human Rights Council—after which the Russian military carried out the most extensive attacks in months—reach 50 in April with all but India and China in favor of a motion ensuring any such body would agree to an immediate resolution of the fighting.

Why is there what appears to be a fatalistic indifference or, indeed, active opposition to the Western stance on the Ukraine war? Beyond the more banal characterisation of these attitudes as “pro-Russian”, this paper attempts to understand how the attitudes of countries in the Global South might be understood, and therefore how the war is being perceived in that context. It therefore divides into three sections. First, it summarises the criticisms of the Western attitude to the war that have been emanating from a number of countries in the Global South and explains the basis of the charge that sanctions against Russia are unfairly prejudicial to the economies of developing countries. Secondly, it examines the extent to which the war has revitalised the old debate about “strategic autonomy” and has, in addition, triggered a debate about “new non-alignment in the 21st century”. Thirdly, it looks at the extent to which the war has highlighted long-standing Global South criticisms of the UN and contributed to the broad movement away from Western-led multilateralism.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
